coredata.dc:title: Convenient electrochemical and fluorometric dual-mode estimation of diosmin using carbon dots doped with nitrogen derived from chicken feet biowaste
coredata.dc:creator: Mahmoud, Ashraf M.
Abu-Alrub, Samer S.
Alqarni, Ali O.
El-Wekil, Mohamed M.
Ali, Al-Montaser Bellah H.
coredata.dc:description: Herein, new carbon dots doped with nitrogen (N.C-dots) were fabricated from chicken feet bio-waste via facile pyrolysis method. It was utilized to determine diosmin (DIS) fluorometrically and electrochemically (dual-mode). Its fluorescence emission was decreased after DIS addition as a result of inner-filtration effect (IFE). In addition, DIS exhibited a redox activity on the carbon paste electrode (CPE) decorated with N.C-dots (N.C-dots/CPE), enabling its detection via electrochemical method. Different techniques were used to characterize the sensing interfaces. There was a linear decrease in the fluorescence emission of N.C-dots at λem. = 430 nm with increasing the amount of DIS over the range of 0.01–0.6 µg mL−1, while there was a linear increase in the oxidation current (Ipa) of DIS with increasing its amount over the range of 0.1–12 µg mL−1. The detection limits (LODs) were calculated to be 3.12 ng mL−1, and 32.91 ng mL−1 for the fluorometric and electrochemical methods, respectively. The as-synthesized sensor possesses many advantages including simplicity, cost-effectiveness, long-term stability, excellent sensitivity, and good selectivity. These advantages enabled the sensor(s) to be applied successfully for the dual-mode detection of DIS in human plasma samples with acceptable results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Diosmin
Dual-mode
N.C-dots
Inner-filtration
Redox-activity
Human plasma

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Instrumentation, calculation of quantum yield, and human plasma preparation
Fluorometric and electrochemical measurement of DIS
Mechanism of fluorescence sensing of DIS using N.C-dots
Electrochemical behavior of DIS at N.C-dots /CPE
Mechanism of DIS oxidation at N.C-dots/CPE
Determination of DIS in human plasma

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are thankful to the Deanship of Scientific Research at Najran University for funding this work under the National Research Priorities funding program grant code (NU/DRP/MRC/12/21).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (A), FT-IR (B), and XRD pattern (C) of the as-fabricated N.C-dots.
(A) Absorption and fluorescence spectra of N.C-dots and (B) dependency of emission wavelengths on the excitation wavelengths.

(A) The influence of different DIS concentrations (0.01 – 0.6 μg mL−1) on the fluorescence emission of N.C-dots. Inset: Plot of (F0-F) versus DIS concentration; (B) The influence of adding increasing amounts of DIS at N.C-Dots over the range of 0.1 – 12.0 μg mL−1. Inset: calibration plot between Ipa and concentration.

(A) The F0/F of N.C-dots in the presence of DIS (10 ng mL−1) and N.C-dots in the presence of co-existing substances (100 folds), (B) Ip of DIS alone (10 μg mL−1) and in presence of co-existing substances 100 folds higher under optimum conditions. The standard deviation of three separate experiments is represented by the error bars.
Main steps for fabrication of sensing interfaces and dual-mode detection of DIS.
Analytical performance results for the determination of DIS.
Determination of DIS in spiked human plasma (n = 3).
Comparison between reported and proposed sensors for determination of DIS.

row.entry: Graphene oxide nanoribbons (GONRs) and electro-reduced GONRs/DPV, SWV

head.ce:title: Convenient electrochemical and fluorometric dual-mode estimation of diosmin using carbon dots doped with nitrogen derived from chicken feet biowaste

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Chemistry, College of Pharmacy, Najran University, Najran, Saudi Arabia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Chemistry, College of Pharmacy, Najran University, Najran, Saudi Arabia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Assiut University, Assiut, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Assiut University, Assiut, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, new carbon dots doped with nitrogen (N.C-dots) were fabricated from chicken feet bio-waste via facile pyrolysis method. It was utilized to determine diosmin (DIS) fluorometrically and electrochemically (dual-mode). Its fluorescence emission was decreased after DIS addition as a result of inner-filtration effect (IFE). In addition, DIS exhibited a redox activity on the carbon paste electrode (CPE) decorated with N.C-dots (N.C-dots/CPE), enabling its detection via electrochemical method. Different techniques were used to characterize the sensing interfaces. There was a linear decrease in the fluorescence emission of N.C-dots at λem. = 430 nm with increasing the amount of DIS over the range of 0.01–0.6 µg mL−1, while there was a linear increase in the oxidation current (Ipa) of DIS with increasing its amount over the range of 0.1–12 µg mL−1. The detection limits (LODs) were calculated to be 3.12 ng mL−1, and 32.91 ng mL−1 for the fluorometric and electrochemical methods, respectively. The as-synthesized sensor possesses many advantages including simplicity, cost-effectiveness, long-term stability, excellent sensitivity, and good selectivity. These advantages enabled the sensor(s) to be applied successfully for the dual-mode detection of DIS in human plasma samples with acceptable results.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (C-Dots) are very interesting nanomaterials in the fields of sensing as a result of their substantial photoluminescence intensity, chemical stability, excellent biocompatibility, non-toxicity, and ease of production from low-cost precursors. Furthermore, they are highly soluble in water, have diameters less than 10 nm and their surface are easily modified [1–3]. Because of their unique properties, they are thought to be the most viable substitute for carbon containing materials [4,5]. Unlike traditional types, which were formed from hazardous chemicals [6], new C-Dots alternative precursors were made from eco-friendly precursors such as starch, cellulose and DNA [7–9]. The use of biomass waste precursors for C-Dots synthesis also fulfills waste recycling necessities. The waste materials and disposals produced by the food processing industry are diverse. By utilizing food waste and high-value biomolecules like proteins, peptides, polysaccharides, carbohydrates, fat, and other biologically active compounds, fluorescent products may be produced [10]. The untreated disposal of chicken feet endangers the ecosystem. The poultry processing industry supplies chicken feet at a rate of about 3.9 million metric tons annually [11]. Poultry by-products contain a high percentage of protein, and chicken feet which contain a high protein percentage, 85 % collagen as protein and 2.7 % fat [12]. Collagen obtained from chicken feet is composed of 10–13% alanine and glutamic acid, 30% glycine and 12% proline [13]. One potential source of C-Dots is the thermal breakdown of dietary proteins. Because biomass waste is protein-rich and environmentally friendly, it is frequently utilized as a starting material to produce C-Dots [14]. Furthermore, using biomass waste to make valuable C-Dots can lower preparation costs, limit ecological harm, and open up new waste management options [15]. As a result, investigation of new types of biomass waste for the production of quantum dots is necessary.

Diosmin (DIS) is employed for treating hemorrhoids as well as blood vessel disorders including lymphedema, venous insufficiency, and varicose veins [16]. Its pharmacological effects include anti-inflammatory [17], antioxidant [18], anxiolytic [19], and antidiabetic effects [20]. Spectrophotometric [21], chromatographic [22–24], capillary electrophoresis [25,26] and voltammetric [27–34] techniques have been employed for determining DIS concentration in different matrices. Some of these methods require high cost complicated operation and there are still some deficiencies such as high detection limits. Determination of analytes using fluorometric or electrochemical methods has proven to be an optimal choice due to the high sensitivity and efficiency of these techniques. Dual-mode analysis have gained popularity in analyte analysis over single-mode due to their efficiency and reliability, and these are examples of previously published methods in which the dual analysis was used; fluorescence/colorimetric [35], and electrochemical/fluorescence [36–38]. Referring to the literature, we found that there is only one technique for determining DIS in its dosage form [39], and this method did not have the appropriate sensitivity to determine the concentration of DIS in blood plasma as Cmax of DIS in blood about 50 ng mL−1 [40]. Therefore, developing sensitive sensors is important for detection of DIS.

Inspired by above reports, a fluorometric and electrochemical dual-mode sensing strategy was developed for reliable estimation of DIS. The fluorometric detection depends on decreasing the fluorescence of N.C-dots via IFE, while the electrochemical quantitation depends on measuring the Ipa of DIS at N.C-dots/CPE. Moreover, the sensors were applied successfully for the determination of DIS in plasma samples.

Diosmin, quinine sulfate, graphite powder, uric acid, dopamine, cysteine, adenine, ascorbic acid, and guanine were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (Steinheim, Germany). Ethanol, sodium hydroxide, glucose, lactose, and sucrose were purchased from Fluka (Buchs, Switzerland). H3PO4, CH3COOH, H3BO4, Na2HPO4 and NaH2PO4 were purchased from Merck (Cairo, Egypt). Analytical grade reagents and chemicals were used along the study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Instrumentation, calculation of quantum yield, and human plasma preparation
ce:section.ce:para: Details were placed in ESM.

It was fabricated via pyrolysis procedure as follow: 100 g of chicken feet biowaste were weighed and placed in a crucible and kept at 300 °C for 5 h. Then, the oven is cooled down to the ambient temperature followed by crushing the resultant powder before sieving with a 1 mm mesh. The collected powder was dispersed in double distilled water (DDW) before filtration using dialysis bag of MWCO = 3000 Da. After 24 h of freeze-drying, the N.C-dots solid was obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorometric and electrochemical measurement of DIS
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorometric method, 1.0 mL of N.C-dots (0.1 mg mL−1) was mixed with 2.5 mL B.R. buffer (pH 7.0), and different volumes of DIS. After that, the volume was completed to 10.0 mL with DDW before incubation at ambient temperature for 1.0 min. The fluorometric measurement of the final solutions were performed at λem. = 430 nm after excitation at 335 nm (Scheme 1 ).

Electrochemical method, the N.C-dots/CPE was fabricated by blending graphite powder, N.C-dots, and paraffin oil in the ratio of 80:10:10, w/w/w %, respectively for 15 min, which were uniformly packed into Teflon tube (id = 3 mm) connected to the copper wire from the opposite side to complete the electrical circuit. After that, the N.C-dots/CPE was inserted into a voltammetric cell containing B.R. buffer (pH 3.0). The accumulation potential for square wave voltammetry (SWV) studies were chosen to be 0.0 V and the optimum accumulation time was found to be 120 s. The solution was allowed to become stagnant for 10 s after the accumulation period before the voltammetric scan. The stirrer was then turned off (Scheme 1).

Chicken feet are the single precursor used in the production of N.C-dots. They have a high percentage of various amino acids, and therefore COOH and NH2 are the main functional groups. The quantum yield was estimated to be 42.9 %. The nanoparticles are well-dispersed and spherical in shape and this confirmed by TEM (Fig. 1 A). The size distribution curve demonstrates a distribution of N.C-dots within 1–10 nm range with a mean diameter of 5.1 nm (Fig. S1). The FT-IR spectroscopic analysis was executed to identify the formation of the N.C-dots ( Fig. 1B). The characteristic band at 3365 cm−1 indicates the ν (OH/NH) while bands at 2878 cm−1 and 2976 cm−1 were attributed to the ν (C–H). The appearance of a strong band at 1651 cm−1 indicates the presence of C = O, while the band at 1431 cm−1 is attributed to ν (C = C). The ν (C-O) and ν (C-N) were noticed at 1380 cm−1 and 1414 cm−1, respectively. Moreover, the absorption bands at 1100 cm−1 and 1051 cm−1 were attributed to δ (C–O) and δ (C-N), respectively [41,42]. The crystal structure of N.C-dots was examined using XRD in Fig. 1C. It was found that the as-prepared carbon dots explore diffraction peak at ∼24.2°, confirming the presence of (002) diffraction plane of graphite [43,44].

The as-fabricated N.C-dots exhibit absorption peaks at 227 nm and 280 nm, referring to C = C and C = O, respectively. Its aqueous solution appeared as light-brown in the natural lighting and emitted a blue fluorescence when excited at 365 nm. Moreover, the emission fluorescence band of N.C-dots is positioned at 430 nm after excitation at 335 nm (Fig. 2 A). The emission spectra of the as-produced carbon dots at various excitation wavelengths were investigated in Fig. 2B. The fluorescence emission increased upon increasing the excitation wavelength until optimum at 335 nm, and then gradually declined. This observation could be ascribed to the different size distribution of N.C-dots [45]. As a result, 335 nm was taken as an optimum excitation wavelength during the fluorometric sensing of DIS using the N.C-dots.

The stability of N.C-dots was investigated in its aqueous solution. During the 20 days of monitoring, it was noticed that the N.C-dots were not subjected to any precipitation, the aqueous solution stays homogeneous, and the fluorescence intensity shows no noticeable change. As a result, the N.C-dots have a reasonable shelf life in their solution. To assess the photolysis sensitivity of N.C-dots, their solution was subjected to UV irradiation for 3 h. The fluorescence of the as-synthesized N.C-dots remained steady over the observation period, indicating that the N.C-dots have good photostability. Fig. S2A shows the impact of irradiation time on the fluorescence of N.C-dots. It was concluded that the N.C-dots had excellent fluorescence with time and no quenching occurs within irradiation period. The pH value can affect the fluorescence intensity of carbon dots as their surface functional groups including –NH2 and –COOH. The charge of these groups can be changed when pH value is altered due to the protonation and deprotonation, thereby affecting their fluorescence intensity. The influence of pH on the emission intensity of N.C-dots is illustrated in Fig. S2B. The greatest fluorescence intensity was observed at pH 7.0. So, pH 7.0 was found as the ideal pH value to get the best sensor sensitivity. Ionic strength is an additional significant factor that influences carbon dots fluorescence emission. Therefore, the fluorescence of N.C-dots containing varied concentrations of NaCl was measured to investigate the effect of ionic strength on the emission of N.C-dots. As shown in Fig. S2C, the fluorescence is unaffected by increasing the NaCl concentration. To conclude, the stability of N.C-dots was achieved in various pH environments, high ionic strength, and different irradiation times.

The addition of N.C-dots to the graphite paste had a significant improvement on the surface area and conductivity. The cyclic voltammetric (CV) scans of the CPE and N.C-dots/CPE were taken in solution containing 1.0 mmol·L−1 Fe(CN)6 -3/-4 and 0.5 mol L−1 KCl. Fig. S3A shows that the current signal (Ipa) of N.C-dots/CPE is higher than bare CPE, referring to the good conductivity and large surface area of N.C-dots/CPE. Moreover, the N.C-dots/CPE has resistance (182.3 Ω) lower than the CPE (229.5 Ω), indicating that the charge transfer resistance at N.C-dots/CPE is lower than CPE (Fig. S3B) [46]. As a result, the N.C-dots/CPE is superior to the unmodified CPE during electrochemical oxidation of DIS. In addition, the surface area of CPE and N.C-dots/CPE were calculated [47]. Surface areas of CPE and N.C-dots /CPE were calculated as 0.133 cm2 and 0.160 cm2, respectively (Fig. S4). The surface area of N.C-dots/CPE is superior to the bare CPE, showing that the N.C-dots /CPE can be effectively used for the electrochemical oxidation of DIS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of fluorescence sensing of DIS using N.C-dots
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence intensity of N.C-dots was significantly reduced after reaction with DIS (Fig. S5). Therefore, the N.C-dots have the potential to be used as a strong fluorescent sensor for the estimation of DIS. Fluorophore which is modified with different functional groups such as NH2 and COOH can be quenched through different physicochemical mechanisms such as rearrangements of molecules, transfer of energy, ground state complexes formation, interactions between molecules, excited-state interactions, and collisions [48]. Different quenching mechanisms may be the reason for decreasing fluorescence intensity such as FRET, IFE, PET, static quenching, and dynamic quenching. When the fluorophore and quencher interact, this results in the formation of non-fluorescent complex in the ground state, it is termed as “static quenching”, while transfer of energy through collisions between them where the fluorophore returns to the ground state, this is termed as dynamic. Another mechanism is inner filter effect (IFE), which requires the overlapping of the absorption spectrum with excitation/emission spectra of fluorophore [49]. The as-fabricated N.C-dots show strong excitation band at 335 nm, while the emission of N.C-dots appeared at 430 nm. DIS exhibits two absorption bands at 372 and 265 nm. It is shown that the excitation and emission spectra of N.C-dots coincided with the absorption spectrum of DIS, which implies that the excitation wavelength and the emitted light of N.C-dots are absorbed by DIS as presented in Fig. S6. As a result, it is plausible to assume that the IFE is the mechanism of N.C-dots fluorescence inhibition. The quenching mechanism of N.C-dots by DIS was investigated by Stern-Volmer analysis [50] that has been performed at different temperatures.
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K sv
Q
where F0 and F are N.C-dots fluorescence emission in the absence and presence of DIS, respectively, Ksv is the quenching constant and Q is the concentration of DIS. F0/F vs DIS concentration plots at various temperature settings are presented in Fig. S7A. It was estimated that the Ksv at 293 K, 313 K, and 333 K were 116.31 × 10-3 L mol−1, 99.41 × 10-3, and 76.24 × 10-3 mL ng−1, respectively. The values of Ksv decreased with increasing the temperature, so it was concluded that, the quenching of fluorescence intensity is static quenching. Moreover, the values of fluorescence lifetimes did not significantly change before and after addition of DIS, suggesting that the quenching is attributed to static mode [51], (Fig. S7B). Based on the results obtained, it is assumed that the synergistic effects of IFE and static quenching led to fluorescence quenching. Moreover, the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of N.C-dots differs from that of the N.C-dots -DIS mixture in terms of absorbance intensity and absorption peaks position. The spectra of DIS found to be red shifted from 372 nm to 392 nm at the second maximum absorption wavelength of DIS and the absorption intensity are raised with increasing concentration of DIS (Fig. S8). The “Hard and Soft Acids and Bases theory” (HSAB) was applied to confirm the generation of a non-fluorescent ground state complex amongst fluorophore and quencher [52]. According to HSAB theory, soft acids are represented by phenolic OH groups in the chemical structure of DIS and the presence of donor atom with a comparatively high polarity (i.e. N atom) on the surface of N.C-dots belong to soft bases category [53]. The phenolic OH (soft acid) of DIS, may interact strongly with soft bases of N.C-dots (the NH2 groups) and form conjugation of N.C-dots -DIS complex. This combination may prevent the non-radiative electron transfer and result in decreasing the fluorescent molecule concentration. Moreover, the transition complex N.C-dots will be strengthened even more by establishing hydrogen bonds and π-π stacking interactions between conjugated system of N.C-dots and DIS aromatic ring (Scheme S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical behavior of DIS at N.C-dots /CPE
ce:section.ce:para:
Electrochemical oxidation of DIS takes place effectively at N.C-dots /CPE in comparison with the bare CPE as presented in Fig. S9. The ability of the N.C-dots /CPE to improve the response of DIS depends mainly on the quantity of N.C-dots added to the graphite paste. The response was amplified by raising the N.C-dots /CPE concentration, and after reaching the highest response in the presence of the appropriate amount (3.0 mg), any further increase after which a decrease in response occurs (Fig. S10). It is possible that the reason for the decrease in the signal after adding certain quantity is increasing number of functional groups available on the surface of N.C-dots, which may result in a steric hindrance by the sugar moiety making interaction with DIS difficult. The pH influence on the electrical signal of DIS at N.C-dots /CPE was investigated by SWV over the pH range 3.0–7.0. The SWV showed two well-defined anodic peaks (P1 and P2) at the employed pH range. The potential of DIS peaks was shifted to negative values as the pH of the solution was raised (Fig. S11A). According to the obtained slopes, −0.0510 for (P1), −0.0697 for (P2), the electrons number appeared for P1 or P2 are equal to the number of the protons [54,55]. These relations between Ep and pH (Fig. S11B and S11C) can be illustrated by the following equations:
Ep
P 1
V
= - 0.051 pH + 0.96
n = 7
and r
= - 0.9877
Ep
P 2
V
= - 0.069 pH + 1.44
n = 7
and r
= - 0.9960

Additionally, it became apparent that the measurements using the B.R. buffer under pH 3.0 provided the highest peak current. Therefore, pH 3.0 was selected as the optimum solution pH in following experiments. The highest anodic currents were attained after a 120 s accumulation time (Fig. S12). Thus, 120 s was selected as an accumulation time for the study. Additionally, the most appropriate values for step height, frequency and pulse height are 10 mV, 150 Hz and 10 mV, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of DIS oxidation at N.C-dots/CPE
ce:section.ce:para:
In this work, the mechanism of DIS oxidation is explained more precisely and in detail than the other proposed oxidation mechanisms which discussed before. In addition, the electrons involved in the electrochemical oxidation of each peak was correctly estimated for the first time, as the previously proposed mechanism did not give the rational number of electrons in the oxidation process, nor did it explain in detail the origin of these electrons [27,28]. Also, some of these studies lacked an explanation for all the peaks that appear during the CV scan, as some of them explained only three or four of these peaks, and therefore our proposed mechanism was presented as the most suitable explanation for DIS oxidation. The effect of multiple CV on the response of DIS at N.C-dots /CPE was used to explain the reaction mechanism of DIS on the designed sensor. Taking into account the findings of earlier studies on the voltammetric behavior of DIS and results obtained in Fig. S13 and Scheme S2, a suitable explanation for its electrochemical behavior has been suggested. Anodic peak (P1) at 0.75 V is attributed to the electrochemical oxidation of the 5′-OH at the B-ring producing o-quinone and p-quinone, which also take part in quasi reversible redox reactions producing peaks P3–P’3 and P4–P’4, respectively. These peaks are quasi-reversible as the anodic peak–cathodic peak separation potential was estimated to be 0.1 V, which is larger than 0.059/n V, and current ratio of peaks P3–P’3 and P4–P’4 was near 1.0. Ortho and para quinone peaks, corresponding to the DIS oxidation products are reduced into the corresponding (peak P’3 and P’4), respectively. The oxidation of the 5-OH group (ring A) was responsible for the peak (P2) at 1.20 V. The decline in DIS anodic peaks P1 and P2 and the raise in the peaks related to the redox couples P3 and P4 (peaks P3–P’3 and P4–P’4, respectively) upon multiple CVs, indicating successful adsorption of DIS oxidation products on the modified electrode surface. The proposed oxidation mechanism was confirmed based on a published paper on the oxidation of 2-methoxyphenol to catechol [56], which is the same moiety that is oxidized in the B ring of DIS. This research is considered as confirmation of the proposed oxidation mechanism as it shows the same cyclic voltammogram but without the appearance of the second peak that is present in DIS. Also, the electron involved in the oxidation steps was confirmed by the correlation between log ν and peak potential as will be discussed later. The scan rate effect on the anodic peaks (P1 and P2) of DIS was investigated using CV scan in the range of 0.05–0.5 Vs−1 (Fig. S14A). The potentials of all peaks were shifted to higher values when the scan rates were increased. The peak potentials (Ep1) and (Ep2) have a good linear relationship with log ν (Fig. S14B):
E
P 1
V
= 0.088
log ν
V
s
- 1
+ 0.81
R 2
= 0.9993
E
P 2
V
= 0.10
log ν
V
s
- 1
+ 1.24
R 2
= 0.9993

The electrons consumed in the oxidation for the first and second peaks of DIS at 0.75 V and 1.20 V were calculated [57]. The number of electrons transferred was found to be 1.0 electron for the first (P1) or second (P2) oxidation peak, which is consistent with the proposed mechanism of DIS oxidation. For the P1, when we plot the log scan rate (log υ) vs the log of peak current (log Ip), linear relationship was found as follows:
log
I
P 1
μ A
= 0.68 log ν
V
s
- 1
+ 1.9721
R 2
= 0.9726

Moreover, correlation coefficient for Ipa dependence on the ν2 (R2 = 0.9669) is slightly higher than on the scan rate (R2 = 0.9511), indicated that the electro-chemical oxidation of DIS at N.C-dots /CPE is governed by diffusion-controlled process and adsorption also happens to some extent. The slope value of 0.68 indicated a diffusion-controlled process accompanied with adsorption process at the proposed sensor [58]. For the P2, the findings showed that DIS exhibits good linearity between Ip and ν1/2, demonstrating the substantial role of diffusion in the electrode reaction of DIS. This suggestion was supported by data analysis through a plot of log Ip versus log ν ( Fig. S14C), and the correlation can be illustrated by the following equation:
log I
P 2
μ A
= 0.51
log
ν
Vs
- 1
+ 2.36
R
2
= 0.9692
This means that the P2 electrode reaction can be considered as an adsorption-control mechanism.

The reproducibility of the as-prepared N.C-Dots was investigated fluorometrically and electrochemically. Six different sensors were prepared independently under the same fabrication conditions and were used for the determination of 0.25 μg mL−1 DIS. It was found that the RSD % values for the fluorometric and electrochemical methods are 3.36 % and 2.89 %, respectively, proving the reproducibility of the as-fabricated sensors [59]. In addition, the stability was assessed by measuring the relative activity (% RA) of the proposed fluorometric and electrochemical sensors. It was found that the % RA did not appreciably change for 26 days, affirming long-term stability.

The designed sensor's selectivity was investigated with the addition of commonly interfering substances including glucose, sucrose, and lactose as well as components that were found in biological fluids like guanine, ascorbic acid, cysteine, uric acid, adenine, and dopamine. DIS is generally co-administered with other medications for treatment of varicose veins or hemorrhoids such as aspirin, acetaminophen, ibuprofen, and hydrocortisone; it was found that they did not interfere with the method when a hundredfold excess concentration of such interference-causing substances was added. As can be seen from Fig.4 A, the influence of co-existing species on the analytical signal of N.C-dots is negligible, indicating high selectivity of N.C-dots-based fluorescence sensor for DIS detection. For the electrochemical method, it was found that 100 folds excess concentration of the aforementioned interfering substances had no impact on the Ip of DIS (10.0 μg mL−1) considerably. As a result, the suggested electrochemical approach can quantify DIS without being influenced by other compounds (Fig.4B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of DIS in human plasma
ce:section.ce:para: The previous data of the proposed methods indicate the ability and the sensitivity of these methods to detect DIS in spiked human plasma. Results presented in Table 2 indicate good recoveries ranging from 97.53 % to 99.26 %, supporting the effective use of the suggested approaches for DIS measurement in complex biological matrices.

The linearity and LOD of the as-synthesized sensor (s) was/were evaluated against previously published methods for DIS measurement (Table 3 ). It was found that the suggested fluorometric and electrochemical methods have sufficient sensitivity toward the detection of DIS, allowing its determination in real samples.

A dual-mode sensing strategy using fluorometric and electrochemical methods was utilized for sensitive and selective determination of DIS in human plasma. This study is the first to determine DIS by virtue of its ability to quench fluorescence of N.C-dots produced from chicken feet biowaste. The proposed electroanalytical method for the determination of DIS was performed on CPE modified with N.C-dots and the mechanism of DIS oxidation was discussed in detail. The methods have been effectively applied to human plasma, with the recoveries of 97.53 % to 99.26 % and RSD % did not exceed 1.47 %. The most important advantages of the proposed methods are sufficient sensitivity, eco-friendly, simplicity, low cost, and wide linearity range.

Ashraf M. Mahmoud: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Samer S. Abu-Alrub: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Ali O. Alqarni: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Mohamed M. El-Wekil: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Software, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Al-Montaser Bellah H. Ali: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Software, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful to the Deanship of Scientific Research at Najran University for funding this work under the National Research Priorities funding program grant code (NU/DRP/MRC/12/21).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.108929.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Principles, mechanisms, and application of carbon quantum dots in sensors: a review
Anal. Methods

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.J. Molaei, Principles, mechanisms, and application of carbon quantum dots in sensors: a review, Analytical Methods, 12 (2020) 1266-1287.

Nature

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Sanderson, Quantum dots go large: a small industry could be on the verge of a boom, Nature, 459 (2009) 760-762.
